county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Missoula County. Named for Father Anthony Ravalli, Jesuit missionary. Bitterroot Valley.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ravalli County Clerk and Recorder in Hamilton holds records from 1893. The Bitterroot Valley was one of Montana's earliest settled areas — St. Mary's Mission (est. 1841 by Jesuits among the Salish) is the oldest continuous settlement in Montana. Mission records at Gonzaga University's Oregon Province Archives predate territorial government. Marcus Daly established the Bitterroot Stock Farm near Hamilton in 1889; Daly family and farm records at MHS. The Ravalli County Museum has local genealogy files. Hamilton was also home to the Rocky Mountain Laboratory (1928+); NIH/PHS personnel records may document researchers. The Bitterroot National Forest records at NARA Seattle identify homesteaders and forest users. Check Missoula County for pre-1893 records. Fort Owen (1850) records at MHS document the earliest European-American settlement.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1893 to present. County Clerk and Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1893. County Clerk and Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1943.

Microfilmed county records including deeds, marriages, and probate. Browse-only image collections from Ravalli County Clerk and Recorder's office.
Federal population schedules for Ravalli County. Montana Territory enumerated from 1870. Agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide additional family details.
Historical newspapers from Ravalli County communities. Montana Historical Society Newspaper Archives and Chronicling America have digitized Montana papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1914 from Dawson County. Eastern Montana agricultural center on the Yellowstone River near the North Dakota border.
county_metadata.research_tips: Richland County Clerk and Recorder in Sidney has records from 1914. Sidney grew as a Northern Pacific Railroad town and sugar beet farming center — Holly Sugar and Great Western Sugar Company employment records identify seasonal workers including Volga German, Mexican, and Japanese laborers. The MonDak Heritage Center in Sidney has local genealogy collections. Check Dawson County for pre-1914 records. The Yellowstone River irrigated farmland attracted settlers from the Upper Midwest. Fort Union Trading Post (across the Missouri River) records at NARA document early fur trade era families. BLM's GLO records document homestead patents. The Bakken oil boom (2008+) brought a new wave of workers; oil industry records at the Montana Oil and Gas Conservation Division. The Sidney Herald newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Sheridan County. Contains the Fort Peck Indian Reservation (Assiniboine and Sioux).
county_metadata.research_tips: Roosevelt County Clerk and Recorder in Wolf Point has records from 1919. Fort Peck Indian Reservation (Assiniboine and Sioux tribes) covers most of the county — BIA Fort Peck Agency records at NARA Denver include Indian census rolls (1885-1940), allotment records, school attendance (Poplar and Wolf Point schools), and agency correspondence. Check Sheridan County for pre-1919 records. Fort Peck Dam (1933-1940) construction brought thousands of workers; Army Corps of Engineers records at NARA document construction workers and their families. The Great Northern Railway brought homesteaders along the Hi-Line. The Wolf Point Herald-News newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS. Fort Peck town site records document the dam-era community. The Roosevelt County Museum in Wolf Point has local family files.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1901 from Custer County. Contains the Northern Cheyenne Indian Reservation.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rosebud County Clerk and Recorder in Forsyth has records from 1901. The Northern Cheyenne Indian Reservation covers part of the county — BIA Tongue River Agency records at NARA Denver include census rolls, allotment records, and school files. The Northern Cheyenne fought for their own reservation separate from the Crow; tribal records and BIA correspondence at NARA document this history. Check Custer County for pre-1901 records. The Rosebud County Museum in Forsyth has ranching-era family files. Coal mining at Colstrip (1920s+) and later strip mining brought workers; mine company records identify employees. The Battle of the Rosebud (1876) military records at NARA document soldiers. Northern Pacific Railroad brought settlers along the Yellowstone valley. The Forsyth newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1906 from Missoula County. Named for Wilbur Fisk Sanders, Montana politician and vigilante.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sanders County Clerk and Recorder in Thompson Falls has records from 1906. Sanders County is in western Montana's timber belt — lumber company records at MHS and University of Montana archives identify workers. The Thompson Falls Historical Museum has local family files. The Northern Pacific Railroad ran through the Clark Fork valley; railroad land records and employment files at MHS and the Minnesota Historical Society. Check Missoula County for pre-1906 records. The Flathead Indian Reservation covers the northern portion of Sanders County — Salish-Kootenai tribal records and BIA records at NARA Denver cover enrolled members. Hot Springs and Plains were small agricultural communities with distinct settlement histories. The Sanders County Ledger newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS. The Noxon Rapids Dam (1959) displaced some families; Army Corps records at NARA document affected property owners.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from Dawson and Valley counties. Northeastern Montana Hi-Line county.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sheridan County Clerk and Recorder in Plentywood has records from 1913. Sheridan County attracted homesteaders from Scandinavia and the Upper Midwest during the 1909-1918 boom. The county was notable for its radical politics in the 1920s-30s — the Producers News (a Communist Party newspaper) in Plentywood is an unusual genealogical source on microfilm at MHS. Check Dawson and Valley counties for pre-1913 records. The Sheridan County Museum in Plentywood has homestead-era family files. The Great Northern Railway Hi-Line brought settlers; railroad land records at the Minnesota Historical Society. BLM's GLO records document homestead patents. Many claims were abandoned during drought; abandoned homestead files at NARA contain biographical information. Prohibition-era bootlegging across the Canadian border produced court records at the district court.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1881 from Deer Lodge County. Consolidated with Butte as Butte-Silver Bow County in 1977. Butte was one of the world's greatest mining cities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Silver Bow County (Butte-Silver Bow since 1977 consolidation) Clerk and Recorder in Butte holds records from 1881. Butte was the greatest mining city in the West — copper, silver, and zinc mining attracted Irish, Cornish, Finnish, Serbian, Croatian, Italian, Chinese, and other immigrant communities. Anaconda Copper Mining Company records at MHS and University of Montana archives include payroll ledgers, employment records, and union files. The Butte-Silver Bow Public Archives is an exceptional local repository with city directories (1884+), mine employment records, ethnic church records, and Sanborn fire insurance maps. The World Museum of Mining in Butte has mining family collections. The Irish community centered on St. Patrick's Church; parish records are on FamilySearch. The Butte Miners' Union records (1878+) at MHS identify thousands of miners. The Chinese community records at MHS document Butte's Chinatown (destroyed 1940s urban renewal).

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1913 from parts of Sweet Grass, Yellowstone, and Carbon counties. Named for the Stillwater River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Stillwater County Clerk and Recorder in Columbus has records from 1913. The Stillwater Mine (platinum and palladium) is a major modern employer, but the county's genealogical records center on ranching and homesteading. Check Sweet Grass, Yellowstone, and Carbon counties for pre-1913 records. Columbus was a Northern Pacific Railroad town; railroad land records and employment files at MHS. The Museum of the Beartooths in Columbus has local family files and photographs. The Absarokee area attracted ranchers in the late 1800s. Nye and the Stillwater Mining District have mining claim records at BLM. BLM's GLO records document homestead patents. The Stillwater County News newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS. Some Scandinavian and German immigrant families settled the irrigated river valley.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1895 from parts of Meagher, Park, and Yellowstone counties. Named for sweetgrass plants in the area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Sweet Grass County Clerk and Recorder in Big Timber has records from 1895. Big Timber was a major wool-shipping center on the Northern Pacific Railroad — the McLeod-Big Timber area was known as the sheep capital of Montana. Norwegian immigrant families heavily settled the county; Sons of Norway lodge records and Lutheran church records document this community. The Crazy Mountain Museum in Big Timber has local family files. Check Meagher, Park, and Yellowstone counties for pre-1895 records. The Boulder River valley attracted ranchers and miners; mining claim records at BLM. The 1910 flood on the Yellowstone River destroyed some properties; disaster relief and insurance records may document affected families. Big Timber Pioneer newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1893 from Chouteau County. Named for the Teton River. Note: county seat Choteau is spelled differently from Chouteau County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Teton County Clerk and Recorder in Choteau has records from 1893. Choteau was the gateway to the Rocky Mountain Front and the Sun River area. A.B. Hamilton established a trading post here in the 1870s. The Old Trail Museum in Choteau has local family files and dinosaur fossil collections. The Teton River valley attracted ranchers in the 1880s-90s. Check Chouteau County for pre-1893 records. The Nature Conservancy's Pine Butte Swamp Preserve area has historical homestead records at BLM. Bynum and other small communities have distinct settlement histories. The Blackfeet Reservation borders the county to the west; some BIA records at NARA Denver cover cross-boundary families. The Choteau Acantha newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS. Dude ranches along the Rocky Mountain Front operated from the 1920s.
